docs.title: ArchBox
docs.text:
ARCH/GARCH volatility models for financial time series.

ArchBox is a comprehensive Python library for estimating, diagnosing, and forecasting volatility using ARCH/GARCH family models. It provides a unified API covering univariate models (GARCH, EGARCH, GJR, APARCH), multivariate models (DCC, CCC, BEKK), regime-switching models (MS-AR, MS-GARCH), threshold models (SETAR, LSTAR), and risk management tools (VaR, ES, backtesting).

docs.title: Features
docs.text:
• Univariate GARCH: GARCH, EGARCH, GJR-GARCH, APARCH, FIGARCH, IGARCH, GARCH-M, Component
  • Multivariate: DCC, CCC, BEKK, GO-GARCH, DECO
  • Regime-Switching: MS-Mean, MS-AR, MS-VAR, MS-GARCH
  • Threshold: SETAR, LSTAR, ESTAR
  • Risk: VaR (parametric, historical, filtered HS, Monte Carlo), Expected Shortfall, backtesting
  • Distributions: Normal, Student-t, Skewed-t, GED
  • Diagnostics: ARCH-LM, Ljung-Box, Jarque-Bera, QQ-plots
  • CLI: Command-line interface for quick analysis
  • Numba acceleration: Optional JIT compilation for critical loops

docs.title: Quick Example
docs.text:
from archbox import GARCH
from archbox.datasets import load_dataset

# Load data
sp500 = load_dataset('sp500')
returns = sp500['returns']

# Fit GARCH(1,1)
model = GARCH(returns, p=1, q=1)
results = model.fit()
print(results.summary())

# Forecast
forecast = results.forecast(horizon=10)

# Risk measures
from archbox.risk import ValueAtRisk
var = ValueAtRisk(results, alpha=0.05)
print(f"VaR(5%): {var.parametric()[-1]:.4f}")

docs.title: Installation
docs.text:
pip install archbox

For development:

pip install archbox[dev]

docs.title: License
docs.text: MIT License - see LICENSE.

docs.title: VolatilityModel
docs.text:
Bases: ABC

Abstract base class for volatility models.

All volatility models (GARCH, EGARCH, GJR-GARCH, etc.) inherit from this class.

Parameters:

Name Type Description Default endog array - like

Time series of returns.

required mean str

Mean model: 'constant' (demean) or 'zero'.

'constant' dist str

Conditional distribution: 'normal', 'studentt', 'skewt'.

'normal'

Attributes:

Name Type Description endog NDArray[float64]

Returns (demeaned if mean='constant').

nobs int

Number of observations.

dist Distribution

Conditional distribution instance.

volatility_process str

Name of the volatility process.

mu float

Estimated mean (0 if mean='zero').

docs.title: start_params abstractmethod property
docs.text: Initial parameter values for optimization.

docs.title: param_names abstractmethod property
docs.text: Parameter names.

docs.title: num_params abstractmethod property
docs.text: Number of model parameters.

docs.title: transform_params(unconstrained) abstractmethod
docs.text: Transform unconstrained parameters to constrained space.

docs.title: untransform_params(constrained) abstractmethod
docs.text: Transform constrained parameters to unconstrained space.

docs.title: bounds() abstractmethod
docs.text: Parameter bounds for optimizer [(lower, upper), ...].

docs.title: fit(method='mle', starting_values=None, variance_targeting=False, disp=True)
docs.text:
Fit the model via Maximum Likelihood Estimation.

Parameters:

Name Type Description Default method str

Estimation method. Currently only 'mle'.

'mle' starting_values ndarray

Custom starting values. If None, uses self.start_params.

None variance_targeting bool

If True, fix omega = var * (1 - persistence).

False disp bool

Display optimization progress.

True

Returns:

Type Description ArchResults

Fitted model results.

docs.title: loglike(params, backcast=None)
docs.text:
Compute log-likelihood.

Parameters:

Name Type Description Default params ndarray

Model parameters.

required backcast float

Initial variance. If None, computed from data.

None

Returns:

Type Description float

Total log-likelihood.

docs.title: loglike_per_obs(params, backcast=None)
docs.text:
Compute per-observation log-likelihood.

Parameters:

Name Type Description Default params ndarray

Model parameters.

required backcast float

Initial variance.

None

Returns:

Type Description ndarray

Log-likelihood per observation, shape (T,).

docs.title: simulate(n, params, seed=None)
docs.text:
Simulate returns and volatility from the model.

Parameters:

Name Type Description Default n int

Number of observations to simulate.

required params ndarray

Model parameters.

required seed int

Random seed.

None

Returns:

Type Description tuple[ndarray, ndarray]

(returns, conditional_volatility) each shape (n,).

docs.title: ArchResults
docs.text:
Container for fitted ARCH/GARCH model results.

Parameters:

Name Type Description Default model VolatilityModel

The fitted model instance.

required params ndarray

Estimated parameters.

required loglike float

Log-likelihood at optimum.

required sigma2 ndarray

Conditional variance series sigma^2_t.

required se_robust ndarray

Robust (Bollerslev-Wooldridge) standard errors.

required se_nonrobust ndarray

Non-robust (inverse Hessian) standard errors.

required convergence bool

Whether optimization converged.

required

docs.title: persistence()
docs.text:
Compute persistence: sum(alpha_i) + sum(beta_j).

Returns:

Type Description float

Persistence value. Must be < 1 for stationarity.

docs.title: half_life()
docs.text:
Compute half-life of volatility shocks.

Returns:

Type Description float

Number of periods for a shock to decay by half.

docs.title: unconditional_variance()
docs.text:
Compute unconditional (long-run) variance.

Returns:

Type Description float

omega / (1 - persistence).

docs.title: forecast(horizon=1, method='analytic')
docs.text:
Forecast conditional variance h steps ahead.

For GARCH(1,1): E[sigma^2_{T+h}] = sigma^2_inf + (alpha+beta)^{h-1} * (sigma^2_{T+1} - sigma^2_inf)

Parameters:

Name Type Description Default horizon int

Number of steps ahead.

1 method str

Forecast method: 'analytic'.

'analytic'

Returns:

Type Description dict

Dictionary with keys: - 'variance': Forecasted variance, shape (horizon,) - 'volatility': Forecasted volatility (sqrt), shape (horizon,)

docs.title: summary()
docs.text:
Generate formatted summary table.

Returns:

Type Description str

Formatted summary with parameters, SE, t-values, p-values, log-likelihood, AIC/BIC, persistence, and half-life.

docs.title: plot(which='volatility')
docs.text:
Plot conditional volatility or diagnostics.

Parameters:

Name Type Description Default which str

Plot type: 'volatility', 'residuals'.

'volatility'

Returns:

Type Description Figure

The figure object.

docs.title: to_dataframe()
docs.text:
Export parameter estimates as DataFrame.

Returns:

Type Description DataFrame

DataFrame with columns: estimate, std_err, t_value, p_value.

Loaded results.

docs.title: full_diagnostics(results, lags=10, arch_lm_lags=None, lb_lags=None)
docs.text:
Run all diagnostic tests on fitted model results.

Parameters:

Name Type Description Default results ArchResults

Fitted model results. Must have attributes: - resids (or endog): raw residuals - conditional_volatility: sigma_t series Optionally: - scores: score matrix for Nyblom test

required lags int

Default number of lags. Default is 10.

10 arch_lm_lags list[int]

Lag counts for ARCH-LM test. Default is [1, 5, 10].

None lb_lags list[int]

Lag counts for Ljung-Box test. Default is [5, 10, 20].

None

Returns:

Type Description DiagnosticReport

Report with all diagnostic test results.

docs.title: GARCH
docs.text:
Bases: VolatilityModel

GARCH(p,q) model.

sigma^2_t = omega + sum_{i=1}^q alpha_i * eps^2_{t-i} + sum_{j=1}^p beta_j * sigma^2_{t-j}

Parameters:

Name Type Description Default endog array - like

Time series of returns.

required p int

Number of GARCH (lagged variance) terms. Default 1.

1 q int

Number of ARCH (lagged squared residual) terms. Default 1.

1 mean str

Mean model: 'constant' or 'zero'.

'constant' dist str

Conditional distribution: 'normal'.

'normal'

Examples:

>>> from archbox import GARCH
>>> from archbox.datasets import load_dataset
>>> sp500 = load_dataset('sp500')
>>> model = GARCH(sp500['returns'], p=1, q=1)
>>> results = model.fit()
>>> print(results.summary())

docs.title: start_params property
docs.text:
Initial parameters via variance targeting.

Sets alpha=0.08, beta=0.88, and derives omega from the sample variance.

docs.title: param_names property
docs.text: Parameter names: omega, alpha[1], ..., beta[1], ...

docs.title: num_params property
docs.text: Number of parameters: 1 + q + p.

docs.title: transform_params(unconstrained)
docs.text:
Transform unconstrained -> constrained parameters.

  • omega: exp(x) to ensure > 0 (clamped to avoid overflow)
  • alpha, beta: sigmoid to ensure >= 0, scaled so sum < 1

docs.title: untransform_params(constrained)
docs.text: Transform constrained -> unconstrained parameters.

docs.title: bounds()
docs.text: Parameter bounds.

docs.title: DCC
docs.text:
Bases: MultivariateVolatilityModel

Dynamic Conditional Correlation GARCH model.

The DCC model extends CCC by allowing the conditional correlation matrix to vary over time, governed by two parameters (a, b).

Parameters:

Name Type Description Default endog ndarray

Array of shape (T, k) with k return series.

required univariate_model str

Univariate GARCH variant. Default 'GARCH'.

'GARCH' univariate_order tuple[int, int]

(p, q) order for univariate GARCH. Default (1, 1).

(1, 1)

Examples:

>>> import numpy as np
>>> from archbox.multivariate.dcc import DCC
>>> returns = np.random.randn(500, 3) * 0.01
>>> model = DCC(returns)
>>> results = model.fit()
>>> print(results.summary())
References

Engle, R.F. (2002). Dynamic Conditional Correlation: A Simple Class of Multivariate Generalized Autoregressive Conditional Heteroskedasticity Models. Journal of Business & Economic Statistics, 20(3), 339-350.

docs.title: start_params property
docs.text: Initial parameter values: a=0.05, b=0.90.

docs.title: param_names property
docs.text: DCC parameter names.

docs.title: forecast(results, horizon=10)
docs.text:
Forecast H_{T+h} using DCC dynamics.

Q_{T+h} converges to Q_bar: Q_{T+h} = (1 - (a+b)^h) * Q_bar + (a+b)^h * Q_T

Parameters:

Name Type Description Default results MultivarResults

Fitted model results.

required horizon int

Number of steps ahead.

10

Returns:

Type Description dict

Dictionary with 'covariance' and 'correlation' forecasts.

docs.title: CCC
docs.text:
Bases: MultivariateVolatilityModel

Constant Conditional Correlation GARCH model.

The CCC model assumes that the conditional correlation matrix R is constant over time. It is estimated as the sample correlation of standardized residuals from univariate GARCH models.

Parameters:

Name Type Description Default endog ndarray

Array of shape (T, k) with k return series.

required univariate_model str

Univariate GARCH variant. Default 'GARCH'.

'GARCH' univariate_order tuple[int, int]

(p, q) order for univariate GARCH. Default (1, 1).

(1, 1)

Examples:

>>> import numpy as np
>>> from archbox.multivariate.ccc import CCC
>>> returns = np.random.randn(500, 3) * 0.01
>>> model = CCC(returns)
>>> results = model.fit()
>>> print(results.summary())
References

Bollerslev, T. (1990). Modelling the Coherence in Short-Run Nominal Exchange Rates: A Multivariate Generalized ARCH Model. Review of Economics and Statistics, 72(3), 498-505.

docs.title: start_params property
docs.text: No parameters to estimate for CCC.

docs.title: param_names property
docs.text: No parameter names for CCC.

docs.title: constant_correlation property
docs.text: Return the estimated constant correlation matrix R.

docs.title: fit(method='two_step', disp=True)
docs.text:
Fit the CCC-GARCH model.

Parameters:

Name Type Description Default method str

Estimation method. Only 'two_step' supported.

'two_step' disp bool

Display progress.

True

Returns:

Type Description MultivarResults

Fitted model results.

docs.title: forecast(results, horizon=10)
docs.text:
Forecast H_{T+h} for CCC.

For CCC, the correlation forecast is simply R (constant). The covariance forecast uses univariate GARCH variance forecasts.

Parameters:

Name Type Description Default results MultivarResults

Fitted model results.

required horizon int

Number of steps ahead.

10

Returns:

Type Description dict

Dictionary with 'covariance' and 'correlation' forecasts.

docs.title: MarkovSwitchingAR
docs.text:
Bases: MarkovSwitchingModel

Markov-Switching AR(p) model (Hamilton, 1989).

y_t - mu_{S_t} = phi_1 * (y_{t-1} - mu_{S_{t-1}}) + ... + eps_t eps_t ~ N(0, sigma^2_{S_t})

Parameters:

Name Type Description Default endog array - like

Time series of observations, shape (T,).

required k_regimes int

Number of regimes. Default is 2.

2 order int

AR order (number of lags). Default is 4.

4 switching_mean bool

If True, the mean switches between regimes. Default True.

True switching_variance bool

If True, the variance switches between regimes. Default True.

True switching_ar bool

If True, AR coefficients switch between regimes. Default False.

False

Examples:

>>> from archbox.regime.ms_ar import MarkovSwitchingAR
>>> from archbox.datasets import load_dataset
>>> gdp = load_dataset('us_gdp_quarterly')
>>> growth = gdp['growth'].to_numpy()
>>> model = MarkovSwitchingAR(growth, k_regimes=2, order=4)
>>> results = model.fit()
>>> print(results.summary())

docs.title: start_params property
docs.text:
Initial parameter values.

Returns:

Type Description ndarray

Initial parameters.

docs.title: param_names property
docs.text: Parameter names.

docs.title: ValueAtRisk
docs.text:
Value at Risk calculator.

Parameters:

Name Type Description Default results ArchResults

Fitted model results from archbox.

required alpha float

Significance level (e.g., 0.05 for 5% VaR). Default is 0.05.

0.05

Attributes:

Name Type Description results ArchResults

The fitted model results.

alpha float

Significance level.

returns NDArray[float64]

The return series from the fitted model.

conditional_volatility NDArray[float64]

Conditional volatility series sigma_t.

docs.title: parametric(dist='normal', nu=8.0)
docs.text:
Compute parametric VaR.

Parameters:

Name Type Description Default dist str

Distribution: 'normal' or 'studentt'. Default is 'normal'.

'normal' nu float

Degrees of freedom for Student-t. Default is 8.0.

8.0

Returns:

Type Description NDArray[float64]

VaR series, shape (T,). Negative values indicate losses.

Notes

Normal: VaR_alpha = mu + sigma_t * Phi^{-1}(alpha)

Student-t: VaR_alpha = mu + sigma_t * t^{-1}_nu(alpha) * sqrt((nu-2)/nu)

docs.title: historical(window=250)
docs.text:
Compute VaR by Historical Simulation.

Parameters:

Name Type Description Default window int

Rolling window size. Default is 250 (approx. 1 year).

250

Returns:

Type Description NDArray[float64]

VaR series, shape (T,). First window values are NaN.

Notes

VaR_alpha = quantile(r_{t-W+1}, ..., r_t ; alpha)

docs.title: filtered_historical()
docs.text:
Compute VaR by Filtered Historical Simulation (FHS).

Returns:

Type Description NDArray[float64]

VaR series, shape (T,).

Notes

Barone-Adesi et al. (1999): 1. z_t = (r_t - mu) / sigma_t (standardized residuals) 2. VaR_t = mu + sigma_t * quantile(z_1, ..., z_{t-1} ; alpha)

The FHS method combines the GARCH volatility dynamics with the empirical distribution of standardized residuals.

docs.title: monte_carlo(n_sims=10000, horizon=1, seed=None)
docs.text:
Compute VaR by Monte Carlo simulation.

Parameters:

Name Type Description Default n_sims int

Number of simulation paths. Default is 10000.

10000 horizon int

Forecast horizon in periods. Default is 1.

1 seed int

Random seed for reproducibility.

None

Returns:

Type Description NDArray[float64]

VaR estimate(s). If horizon=1, returns scalar-like array. If horizon>1, returns array of shape (horizon,).

Notes
  1. Use the last conditional variance as starting point.
  2. Simulate N paths of sigma^2_{T+h} and r_{T+h}.
  3. VaR_alpha = quantile(simulated returns ; alpha).

docs.title: ExpectedShortfall
docs.text:
Expected Shortfall (CVaR) calculator.

Parameters:

Name Type Description Default results ArchResults

Fitted model results from archbox.

required alpha float

Significance level (e.g., 0.05 for 5% ES). Default is 0.05.

0.05

Attributes:

Name Type Description results ArchResults

The fitted model results.

alpha float

Significance level.

returns NDArray[float64]

The return series.

conditional_volatility NDArray[float64]

Conditional volatility series sigma_t.

docs.title: parametric(dist='normal', nu=8.0)
docs.text:
Compute parametric Expected Shortfall.

Parameters:

Name Type Description Default dist str

Distribution: 'normal' or 'studentt'. Default is 'normal'.

'normal' nu float

Degrees of freedom for Student-t. Default is 8.0.

8.0

Returns:

Type Description NDArray[float64]

ES series, shape (T,). Negative values indicate expected losses.

Notes

Normal: ES_alpha = mu - sigma * phi(z_alpha) / alpha

Student-t: ES_alpha = mu - sigma * (f_nu(t_alpha) / alpha) * ((nu + t_alpha^2) / (nu - 1)) * sqrt((nu-2)/nu)

docs.title: historical(window=250)
docs.text:
Compute ES by Historical Simulation.

Parameters:

Name Type Description Default window int

Rolling window size. Default is 250.

250

Returns:

Type Description NDArray[float64]

ES series, shape (T,). First window values are NaN.

Notes

ES_alpha = mean(r_t | r_t < VaR_alpha) in rolling window.

docs.title: filtered_historical()
docs.text:
Compute ES by Filtered Historical Simulation.

Returns:

Type Description NDArray[float64]

ES series, shape (T,).

Notes
  1. z_t = (r_t - mu) / sigma_t
  2. ES_t = mu + sigma_t * mean(z_s | z_s < quantile(z; alpha))

docs.title: VaRBacktest
docs.text:
VaR Backtesting framework.

Parameters:

Name Type Description Default returns array - like

Realized return series.

required var_series array - like

VaR forecast series (must be negative for losses).

required alpha float

Significance level of the VaR. Default is 0.05.

0.05

Attributes:

Name Type Description returns NDArray[float64]

Realized returns.

var NDArray[float64]

VaR forecasts.

alpha float

Significance level.

hits NDArray[int64]

Hit sequence: 1 if r_t < VaR_t, 0 otherwise.

docs.title: kupiec_test()
docs.text:
Kupiec (1995) Proportion of Failures (POF) test.

Tests H0: violation rate = alpha.

Returns:

Type Description TestResult

LR_POF statistic and p-value, chi2(1).

Notes

LR_POF = -2 * [xlog(alpha) + (n-x)log(1-alpha) - xlog(pi_hat) - (n-x)log(1-pi_hat)] LR_POF ~ chi2(1)

docs.title: christoffersen_test()
docs.text:
Christoffersen (1998) Conditional Coverage test.

Tests H0: violations are independent AND rate = alpha.

Returns:

Type Description TestResult

LR_CC statistic and p-value, chi2(2).

Notes

LR_CC = LR_POF + LR_ind ~ chi2(2)

Where LR_ind tests independence of the hit sequence.

docs.title: basel_traffic_light(window=250)
docs.text:
Basel traffic light system.

Parameters:

Name Type Description Default window int

Backtesting window in days. Default is 250.

250

Returns:

Type Description str

'green', 'yellow', or 'red'.

Notes

For 250 days at alpha=1%: - Green: 0-4 violations - Yellow: 5-9 violations - Red: 10+ violations

docs.title: violation_ratio()
docs.text:
Compute the violation ratio.

Returns:

Type Description float

Observed violation rate / expected violation rate (alpha). A ratio of 1.0 indicates perfect calibration.

docs.title: summary()
docs.text:
Generate a full backtest report.

Returns:

Type Description str

Formatted report with all test results.

docs.title: Targets
docs.text: Scenario Target Backend GARCH(1,1) T=1000 < 100ms Python GARCH(1,1) T=1000 < 10ms Numba GARCH(1,1) T=10000 < 50ms Numba EGARCH(1,1) T=1000 < 15ms Numba DCC (2 series) T=1000 < 500ms Auto DCC (5 series) T=1000 < 2s Auto MS-AR(2,4) T=500 EM < 5s Auto Hamilton filter T=1000 k=2 < 10ms Numba VaR Monte Carlo 10K sims < 5s Auto

docs.title: Enabling Numba
docs.text:
from archbox.utils.backend import set_backend
set_backend('numba')

docs.title: Running benchmarks
docs.text:
pytest tests/benchmarks/test_performance.py -v -s
pytest tests/benchmarks/test_scaling.py -v -s

docs.title: Requirements
docs.text:
• Python >= 3.11
  • NumPy >= 1.24
  • SciPy >= 1.10
  • pandas >= 2.0
  • matplotlib >= 3.7

docs.title: Install from PyPI
docs.text: pip install archbox

docs.title: Install from source
docs.text:
git clone https://github.com/nodesecon/archbox.git
cd archbox
pip install -e ".[dev]"

docs.title: Numba acceleration
docs.text:
For significantly faster model fitting:

pip install archbox[numba]

This enables JIT-compiled inner loops for GARCH recursion, Hamilton filter, and DCC recursion.

docs.title: Documentation
docs.text:
To build the documentation locally:

pip install archbox[docs]
mkdocs serve

docs.title: Verify installation
docs.text:
import archbox
print(f"archbox version: {archbox.__version__}")

from archbox.datasets import load_dataset
sp500 = load_dataset('sp500')
print(f"SP500 dataset: {len(sp500)} observations")

docs.title: Volatility clustering
docs.text: Financial returns exhibit volatility clustering: large changes tend to be followed by large changes, and small changes by small changes. GARCH models capture this phenomenon.

docs.title: The GARCH(p,q) model
docs.text:
The general model:

\[r_t = \mu + \varepsilon_t, \quad \varepsilon_t = \sigma_t z_t, \quad z_t \sim D(0,1)\] \[\sigma^2_t = \omega + \sum_{i=1}^{q} \alpha_i \varepsilon_{t-i}^2 + \sum_{j=1}^{p} \beta_j \sigma^2_{t-j}\]
  • omega: long-run variance component
  • alpha: ARCH effect (reaction to shocks)
  • beta: GARCH effect (persistence)
  • persistence: alpha + beta (should be < 1 for stationarity)

docs.title: Model hierarchy
docs.text: Model Key feature GARCH Symmetric volatility response EGARCH Asymmetric, log-space (no positivity constraint) GJR-GARCH Asymmetric leverage via indicator function APARCH Power-transformed asymmetric DCC Dynamic conditional correlation (multivariate) MS-AR Regime-switching dynamics SETAR Threshold autoregressive

docs.title: Estimation
docs.text: All models are estimated by Maximum Likelihood Estimation (MLE) using SciPy's optimization routines. The log-likelihood depends on the chosen conditional distribution (Normal, Student-t, Skewed-t, GED).

docs.title: Variance targeting
docs.text:
When variance_targeting=True, omega is fixed to satisfy:

\[\omega = \hat{\sigma}^2 (1 - \sum \alpha_i - \sum \beta_j)\]

This reduces the parameter space and can improve convergence.

docs.title: Quickstart
docs.text: This guide walks through a complete volatility analysis workflow.

docs.title: 1. Load data
docs.text:
from archbox.datasets import load_dataset

sp500 = load_dataset('sp500')
returns = sp500['returns'].to_numpy()
print(f"Observations: {len(returns)}")
print(f"Mean return: {returns.mean():.6f}")
print(f"Std return: {returns.std():.6f}")

docs.title: 2. Fit a GARCH model
docs.text:
from archbox import GARCH

model = GARCH(returns, p=1, q=1)
results = model.fit()
print(results.summary())

docs.title: 3. Diagnostics
docs.text:
from archbox.diagnostics import full_diagnostics

diag = full_diagnostics(results)
print(f"ARCH-LM p-value: {diag.arch_lm.pvalue:.4f}")
print(f"Ljung-Box p-value: {diag.ljung_box.pvalue:.4f}")

docs.title: 4. Forecast
docs.text:
forecast = results.forecast(horizon=10)
print(forecast)

docs.title: 5. Risk measures
docs.text:
from archbox.risk import ValueAtRisk, ExpectedShortfall

var = ValueAtRisk(results, alpha=0.05)
var_series = var.parametric()
print(f"Last VaR(5%): {var_series[-1]:.6f}")

es = ExpectedShortfall(results, alpha=0.05)
es_series = es.compute(method='parametric')
print(f"Last ES(5%): {es_series[-1]:.6f}")

docs.title: 6. Compare models
docs.text:
from archbox.experiment import ArchExperiment

exp = ArchExperiment(returns)
exp.fit_all_models([
    ('GARCH', {'p': 1, 'q': 1}),
    ('EGARCH', {'p': 1, 'q': 1}),
    ('GJR', {'p': 1, 'q': 1}),
])
comparison = exp.compare_models()
print(comparison.ranking())
print(f"Best model: {comparison.best_model()}")

docs.title: 7. CLI
docs.text:
archbox estimate --model garch --data sp500.csv --p 1 --q 1 --output results.json
archbox risk --model garch --data sp500.csv --alpha 0.05

docs.title: ARCH Model (Engle, 1982)
docs.text:
The Autoregressive Conditional Heteroskedasticity (ARCH) model:

\[r_t = \mu + \varepsilon_t, \quad \varepsilon_t = \sigma_t z_t, \quad z_t \sim D(0,1)\] \[\sigma^2_t = \omega + \sum_{i=1}^{q} \alpha_i \varepsilon^2_{t-i}\]

docs.title: GARCH Model (Bollerslev, 1986)
docs.text:
The Generalized ARCH model adds lagged conditional variance:

\[\sigma^2_t = \omega + \sum_{i=1}^{q} \alpha_i \varepsilon^2_{t-i} + \sum_{j=1}^{p} \beta_j \sigma^2_{t-j}\]

docs.title: Stationarity condition
docs.text: \[\sum_{i=1}^{q} \alpha_i + \sum_{j=1}^{p} \beta_j < 1\]

docs.title: Unconditional variance
docs.text: \[E[\sigma^2_t] = \frac{\omega}{1 - \sum \alpha_i - \sum \beta_j}\]

docs.title: EGARCH (Nelson, 1991)
docs.text: \[\log(\sigma^2_t) = \omega + \alpha(|z_{t-1}| - E|z|) + \gamma z_{t-1} + \beta \log(\sigma^2_{t-1})\]

docs.title: GJR-GARCH (Glosten et al., 1993)
docs.text: \[\sigma^2_t = \omega + (\alpha + \gamma I_{t-1}) \varepsilon^2_{t-1} + \beta \sigma^2_{t-1}\]

docs.title: APARCH (Ding et al., 1993)
docs.text: \[\sigma^\delta_t = \omega + \alpha(|\varepsilon_{t-1}| - \gamma \varepsilon_{t-1})^\delta + \beta \sigma^\delta_{t-1}\]

docs.title: References
docs.text:
• Bollerslev, T. (1986). Generalized Autoregressive Conditional Heteroskedasticity.
  • Engle, R.F. (1982). Autoregressive Conditional Heteroscedasticity.
  • Nelson, D.B. (1991). Conditional Heteroskedasticity in Asset Returns.
  • Glosten, L.R., Jagannathan, R., & Runkle, D.E. (1993). On the Relation between Expected Value and Volatility.
  • Ding, Z., Granger, C.W.J., & Engle, R.F. (1993). A Long Memory Property of Stock Market Returns.

docs.title: DCC (Engle, 2002)
docs.text:
The Dynamic Conditional Correlation model decomposes the conditional covariance:

\[H_t = D_t R_t D_t\]

where \(D_t = \text{diag}(\sigma_{1,t}, \ldots, \sigma_{k,t})\) and \(R_t\) is the dynamic correlation matrix.

docs.title: CCC (Bollerslev, 1990)
docs.text: The Constant Conditional Correlation model assumes \(R_t = R\) (constant).

docs.title: BEKK (Engle & Kroner, 1995)
docs.text: \[H_t = C'C + A' \varepsilon_{t-1} \varepsilon'_{t-1} A + B' H_{t-1} B\]

docs.title: References
docs.text:
• Engle, R.F. (2002). Dynamic Conditional Correlation.
  • Bollerslev, T. (1990). Modelling the Coherence in Short-Run Nominal Exchange Rates.
  • Engle, R.F. & Kroner, K.F. (1995). Multivariate Simultaneous Generalized ARCH.

docs.title: Markov-Switching (Hamilton, 1989)
docs.text:
The model assumes the data-generating process depends on an unobserved regime \(s_t\):

\[y_t = \mu_{s_t} + \sum_{i=1}^{p} \phi_{i,s_t} y_{t-i} + \sigma_{s_t} \varepsilon_t\]

docs.title: Hamilton filter
docs.text:
The filter computes filtered probabilities \(P(s_t | y_1, \ldots, y_t)\) using:

  1. Prediction: \(\hat{\xi}_{t|t-1} = P' \hat{\xi}_{t-1|t-1}\)
  2. Update: \(\hat{\xi}_{t|t} \propto \hat{\xi}_{t|t-1} \odot f(y_t | s_t, \mathcal{F}_{t-1})\)

docs.title: Kim smoother
docs.text: Backward pass to compute smoothed probabilities \(P(s_t | y_1, \ldots, y_T)\).

docs.title: References
docs.text:
• Hamilton, J.D. (1989). A New Approach to the Economic Analysis of Nonstationary Time Series.
  • Kim, C.-J. (1994). Dynamic Linear Models with Markov-Switching.

docs.title: Value at Risk (VaR)
docs.text: \[\text{VaR}_\alpha = \inf\{x : P(L > x) \leq 1 - \alpha\}\]

docs.title: Methods
docs.text:
1. Parametric: \(\text{VaR}_\alpha = \mu_t + \sigma_t \cdot q_\alpha(D)\)
  2. Historical Simulation: empirical quantile of returns
  3. Filtered Historical Simulation: empirical quantile of standardized residuals, rescaled
  4. Monte Carlo: simulation-based quantile

docs.title: Expected Shortfall (ES)
docs.text: \[\text{ES}_\alpha = E[L | L > \text{VaR}_\alpha]\]

docs.title: Kupiec test (1995)
docs.text:
Tests if violation rate matches expected rate:

\[LR_{uc} = -2 \log\frac{\alpha^n (1-\alpha)^{T-n}}{\hat{p}^n (1-\hat{p})^{T-n}} \sim \chi^2(1)\]

docs.title: Christoffersen test (1998)
docs.text: Tests independence of violations (no clustering).

docs.title: Basel traffic light
docs.text: Zone Violations (at 99%) Action Green 0-4 No action Yellow 5-9 Increase capital Red 10+ Regulatory action

docs.title: References
docs.text:
• Kupiec, P.H. (1995). Techniques for Verifying the Accuracy of Risk Measurement Models.
  • Christoffersen, P. (1998). Evaluating Interval Forecasts.
  • Basel Committee on Banking Supervision (2006). International Convergence of Capital Measurement.

docs.title: Available distributions
docs.text: Distribution Parameter Use case Normal - Default, baseline Student-t nu (degrees of freedom) Heavy tails Skewed-t nu, lambda Asymmetry + heavy tails GED nu (shape) Flexible tail weight

docs.title: Usage
docs.text:
from archbox import GARCH

# Normal (default)
model = GARCH(returns, p=1, q=1, dist='normal')

# Student-t
model = GARCH(returns, p=1, q=1, dist='studentt')

# Skewed-t
model = GARCH(returns, p=1, q=1, dist='skewt')

# GED
model = GARCH(returns, p=1, q=1, dist='ged')

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
The EGARCH (Exponential GARCH) model by Nelson (1991) models log-volatility, naturally ensuring positive variance without parameter constraints.

\[\log(\sigma^2_t) = \omega + \alpha(|z_{t-1}| - E|z_{t-1}|) + \gamma z_{t-1} + \beta \log(\sigma^2_{t-1})\]

docs.title: Usage
docs.text:
from archbox.models.egarch import EGARCH
from archbox.datasets import load_dataset

sp500 = load_dataset('sp500')
model = EGARCH(sp500['returns'], p=1, q=1)
results = model.fit()
print(results.summary())

docs.title: Leverage effect
docs.text: The gamma parameter captures leverage: negative gamma means negative shocks increase volatility more than positive shocks.

docs.title: GARCH(1,1)
docs.text:
The standard GARCH(1,1) model:

from archbox import GARCH
from archbox.datasets import load_dataset

sp500 = load_dataset('sp500')
returns = sp500['returns']

model = GARCH(returns, p=1, q=1)
results = model.fit()
print(results.summary())

docs.title: Higher-order GARCH
docs.text:
model = GARCH(returns, p=2, q=2)
results = model.fit()

docs.title: With Student-t distribution
docs.text:
model = GARCH(returns, p=1, q=1, dist='studentt')
results = model.fit()

docs.title: Variance targeting
docs.text:
model = GARCH(returns, p=1, q=1)
results = model.fit(variance_targeting=True)

docs.title: Forecasting
docs.text:
forecast = results.forecast(horizon=10)
print(forecast)

docs.title: Simulation
docs.text: sim_returns, sim_vol = model.simulate(n=1000, params=results.params, seed=42)

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
The GJR-GARCH model by Glosten, Jagannathan, and Runkle (1993) adds an asymmetric term to capture leverage effects:

\[\sigma^2_t = \omega + (\alpha + \gamma I_{t-1}) \varepsilon^2_{t-1} + \beta \sigma^2_{t-1}\]

where \(I_{t-1} = 1\) if \(\varepsilon_{t-1} < 0\).

docs.title: Usage
docs.text:
from archbox.models.gjr import GJR
from archbox.datasets import load_dataset

sp500 = load_dataset('sp500')
model = GJR(sp500['returns'], p=1, q=1)
results = model.fit()
print(results.summary())

docs.title: DCC (Dynamic Conditional Correlation)
docs.text:
from archbox.multivariate.dcc import DCC
from archbox.datasets import load_dataset

fx = load_dataset('fx_majors')
returns = fx[['usd_eur', 'usd_gbp']].dropna().to_numpy()

model = DCC(returns, p=1, q=1)
results = model.fit()

# Dynamic correlation
dyn_corr = results.dynamic_correlation()

# Portfolio variance
weights = [0.5, 0.5]
port_var = results.portfolio_variance(weights)

docs.title: CCC (Constant Conditional Correlation)
docs.text:
from archbox.multivariate.ccc import CCC

model = CCC(returns)
results = model.fit()
R = results.constant_correlation

docs.title: MS-AR (Markov-Switching Autoregressive)
docs.text:
from archbox.regime.ms_ar import MSAR
from archbox.datasets import load_dataset

gdp = load_dataset('us_gdp')
data = gdp['growth'].dropna().to_numpy()

model = MSAR(data, k_regimes=2, order=4)
results = model.fit(method='em')
print(results.summary())

# Smoothed probabilities
probs = results.smoothed_probabilities()

# Regime classification
regimes = results.classify_regimes()

# Transition matrix
P = results.transition_matrix

docs.title: Interpretation
docs.text:
• Regime 0: typically expansion (higher mean growth)
  • Regime 1: typically recession (lower or negative mean growth)
  • Transition probabilities indicate regime persistence

docs.title: Value at Risk (VaR)
docs.text:
from archbox import GARCH
from archbox.risk import ValueAtRisk
from archbox.datasets import load_dataset

sp500 = load_dataset('sp500')
model = GARCH(sp500['returns'], p=1, q=1)
results = model.fit()

var = ValueAtRisk(results, alpha=0.05)

# Methods
var_param = var.parametric()
var_hist = var.historical()
var_fhs = var.filtered_historical()
var_mc = var.monte_carlo(n_sims=10000)

docs.title: Expected Shortfall (ES)
docs.text:
from archbox.risk import ExpectedShortfall

es = ExpectedShortfall(results, alpha=0.05)
es_series = es.compute(method='parametric')

docs.title: Backtesting
docs.text:
from archbox.risk import VaRBacktest

bt = VaRBacktest(returns[-500:], var_param[-500:], alpha=0.05)
print(f"Violation ratio: {bt.violation_ratio():.4f}")
print(f"Kupiec p-value: {bt.kupiec_test().pvalue:.4f}")
print(f"Traffic light: {bt.traffic_light()}")

docs.title: SETAR (Self-Exciting Threshold Autoregressive)
docs.text:
from archbox.threshold.setar import SETAR
from archbox.datasets import load_dataset

ip = load_dataset('industrial_production')
data = ip['growth'].dropna().to_numpy()

model = SETAR(data, order=2, n_regimes=2)
results = model.fit()
print(results.summary())
print(f"Threshold: {results.threshold}")

docs.title: LSTAR (Logistic Smooth Transition Autoregressive)
docs.text:
from archbox.threshold.lstar import LSTAR

model = LSTAR(data, order=2)
results = model.fit()
transition = results.transition_function()
